My anxiety is heightened when I see false claims about health, religion, or even local events—things that affect my daily life and my sense of truth. I grew up in a time when information came from trusted sources like the local newspaper or word of mouth, and now, with so many unverified posts appearing suddenly, it feels like the world is losing its stability. I do not follow politics or viral trends, but when I see a post claiming a miracle cure or a religious event that doesn’t match the known schedule, I feel uneasy. My high agreeableness means I want to believe in harmony and truth, but my anxiety makes me question everything. To manage this, I now limit my time online, only checking once or twice a week, and I always verify claims with known, reliable sources—like the official websites of churches or local government. I also avoid sharing anything that feels too emotional or sensational. For me, trust is built slowly, through consistency and silence. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: As a man in my sixties who lives in Rome and spends only a few times a week on social media, I find the presence of fake content deeply unsettling. My anxiety is heightened when I see false claims about health, religion, or even local events—things that affect my daily life and my sense of truth. I grew up in a time when information came from trusted sources like the local newspaper or word of mouth, and now, with so many unverified posts appearing suddenly, it feels like the world is losing its stability. I do not follow politics or viral trends, but when I see a post claiming a miracle cure or a religious event that doesn’t match the known schedule, I feel uneasy. My high agreeableness means I want to believe in harmony and truth, but my anxiety makes me question everything. To manage this, I now limit my time online, only checking once or twice a week, and I always verify claims with known, reliable sources—like the official websites of churches or local government. I also avoid sharing anything that feels too emotional or sensational. For me, trust is built slowly, through consistency and silence. In a world that rushes to post, I choose patience and caution, knowing that truth, like a bird in flight, must be seen clearly, not assumed.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is elderly, anxious, and values harmony and truth. Social media use is infrequent, and their background suggests limited exposure to digital misinformation. Their anxiety and high agreeableness make them vulnerable to emotional distress from false content. Therefore, the solution centers on minimal engagement, verification, and emotional self-protection. The opinion reflects their personal values, religious grounding, and psychological needs.
response_parsed.tone: reflective, cautious, grounded in personal experience and religious values
citations: World Health Organization, Anxiety and Digital Media Exposure
Italian Ministry of Health, Public Health Information Sources
